text: We present the case of a sixty-eight-year-old woman who presented with a four-day history of generalized abdominal pain and bilious vomiting. A CT scan revealed small bowel obstruction secondary to an obstructed left obturator hernia.
Using a three-port laparoscopic technique, the surgeon visualizes the obstructed obturator hernia and applies gentle traction to successfully reduce the loop of small bowel.
This diagram depicts the locations of other groin hernias as well as the location of the femoral vein and bladder. This left the conundrum of how to deal with a hernia defect to prevent recurrence.

A small polypropylene mesh, shaped into an hourglass by tying it with a suture, is introduced into the abdomen through the umbilical port. One end of the mesh is inserted into the hernia defect, while the other end is spread open like a fan and secured with metallic clips.

Incidentally, an upper abdominal mass was also found on CT. At laparoscopy, the mass was found to be mobile. A subsequent endoscopic ultrasound raises the possibility of a sarcoma and the patient is currently awaiting an excision biopsy.
